event: Middle Stone Age humans inhabit the region, with the Border Cave in the nearby Lebombo Mountains preserving evidence of some of the earliest anatomically modern human activity.
Lion Cavern at Ngwenya is mined for red ochre and specularite, making it one of the oldest known mining sites in the world.
San hunter-gatherers occupy the region, leaving rock art across the mountains and shelters of present-day Eswatini.
Bantu-speaking agro-pastoralists migrate into the region during the Iron Age, introducing farming, cattle-keeping and ironworking.
Nguni-speaking clans, ancestors of the modern Swazi, settle in southeastern Africa as part of broader southward Bantu migrations.
Ngwane III, regarded as the first king of the modern Swazi nation, leads the Dlamini clan and settles near the Pongola River.
King Ngwane III dies and is later honored as a founding monarch of the Swazi royal lineage.
King Sobhuza I begins his reign, consolidating power and moving the nation northward into the heartland of present-day Eswatini.
Sobhuza I expands the kingdom through conquest and alliance amid the upheavals of the Mfecane, absorbing many smaller Nguni and Sotho clans.
King Mswati II, after whom the Swazi nation takes its name, ascends the throne and builds a powerful centralized military state.
Mswati II organizes the kingdom's regiments and extends Swazi influence far to the north into the Transvaal region.
King Mswati II dies, having forged the territorial and political foundations of the Swazi kingdom.
King Mbandzeni accedes to the throne and presides over a period of growing European pressure for land and mineral concessions.
Britain and the Transvaal South African Republic sign a convention recognizing the independence of Swaziland.
European concession-hunters flood Swaziland, obtaining sweeping grazing, mining and trading rights from King Mbandzeni.
King Mbandzeni dies, leaving the kingdom entangled in a web of overlapping European concessions.
A provisional government of British, Transvaal and Swazi representatives is established to administer the concession-ridden territory.
A convention places Swaziland under the administration of the Transvaal South African Republic as a protected state.
The outbreak of the Second Anglo-Boer War throws Swaziland's administration into uncertainty as Boer control collapses.
Following the British victory in the Anglo-Boer War, Britain takes over administration of Swaziland.
Swaziland becomes a British protectorate administered through the governor of the Transvaal.
A land partition proclamation divides the country, allotting roughly two-thirds to European settlers and concessionaires.
King Sobhuza II begins his reign, launching a decades-long campaign to recover Swazi land lost to colonial concessions.
Sobhuza II establishes a national fund and travels to London to petition the Privy Council over alienated Swazi land.
The Privy Council rejects Sobhuza II's appeal challenging the 1907 land partition, hardening Swazi resolve to buy back land.
A proclamation reaffirms the authority of the Swazi king (Ngwenyama) as the recognized native authority under colonial rule.
Britain promulgates a constitution and Swaziland holds its first elections amid emerging political parties.
Sobhuza II's royalist Imbokodvo National Movement wins all seats in the legislative council, dominating early electoral politics.
Swaziland becomes a self-governing protectorate with Sobhuza II recognized as head of state.
Swaziland gains full independence from Britain on 6 September and joins the United Nations.
Swaziland becomes a member of the Organisation of African Unity, forerunner of the African Union.
King Sobhuza II repeals the constitution, bans political parties and assumes absolute power, ending the multiparty experiment.
Sobhuza II replaces the parliamentary system with the traditional tinkhundla councils as the basis of governance.
A new constitution institutionalizes the tinkhundla system of indirect, non-party elections.
King Sobhuza II dies after one of the longest documented reigns in history, triggering a period of regency and court intrigue.
Queen Regent Dzeliwe is ousted in a palace power struggle and replaced by Queen Regent Ntombi.
Prince Makhosetive is crowned King Mswati III at age eighteen, becoming one of the world's youngest monarchs.
Pro-democracy protests and labor unrest grow, with activists demanding reform of the royal tinkhundla system.
Mass strikes and stay-aways pressure King Mswati III to appoint a commission to review the constitution.
Swaziland is hit by a severe HIV/AIDS epidemic that gives the country one of the highest infection rates in the world.
King Mswati III signs a new constitution that retains royal supremacy and bans political parties while nominally guaranteeing rights.
A fiscal crisis forces Swaziland to seek a loan from South Africa as pro-democracy demonstrations intensify.
King Mswati III renames the country the Kingdom of Eswatini, meaning 'land of the Swazi', marking 50 years of independence.
Pro-democracy protests erupt across Eswatini, met with a deadly security crackdown in the country's worst unrest in decades.
Prominent pro-democracy MP and human rights lawyer Thulani Maseko is assassinated, drawing international condemnation.
